【Day 14】I Installed Cracked Software and Broke My Computer Again

I installed cracked software and broke my computer again

As a student, I went to cafés a lot, but my pocket was thin. School assignments required Microsoft Word, and Google Docs back then wasn't as ubiquitous. Word was a real problem — definitely not cheap.

Microsoft Word is the word processor in Microsoft Office, used for writing, editing, and formatting documents. It's full-featured — text styles, paragraph formatting, image insertion, tables, etc. — and a common, practical tool at school, work, and even daily life.
"Hey, XXX, does your home computer have Word?" I asked a classmate.
"Nah, I use a pirated version~" he laughed.
"Pirated? Can you teach me?" I asked, curious.
"Easy, just search 'Office crack' online." he said.

When I got home, I immediately opened my computer and searched "Office crack" on Yahoo! Search results were full of "no install needed", "permanent license" articles that looked great. I didn't know what viruses or trojans were — I just thought "Wow! Saving money is this easy!" So I picked a tutorial I could follow and downloaded, installed step by step. Looking back, classic case of "broke student + youthful naivete".
this post doesn't endorse or support using cracked software
After install, everything looked fine. I was even proud of having "saved some money". But a few days later, after rebooting, the computer started lagging insanely.
I suspected the network was acting up, ran
to check — normal. I was getting nervous, so I restarted to try my luck. But the real problem was just emerging...
After restart, the computer was fine briefly — relief. But after a bit of use, it started lagging again. By then I had a guess — likely
I ran an antivirus scan immediately. Result: "no virus found". But the lag persisted. At that point I was completely out of options — had to reinstall the whole system.

Cracked software is, as the name says, software that's been modified — e.g., removing built-in mechanisms, or replacing certain files with others, letting users use what would normally be paid software for free.
Install software that's been altered by a third party.
Install the crack itself (commonly Keygen, Patch, etc.).
Configure some settings (e.g., modify registry, tweak system settings).
These installs often sneak strange files into system folders or alter system settings randomly. Result: unstable computer, sometimes total dead machine. Not surprising.
Explaining cracking in detail is hard — different software has very different crack principles. Some use advanced techniques like reverse engineering, code injection, etc.
But we can talk about one thing: the
file. Many crack tools use it.
What does this file do? In short: maps URLs to IPs. When you type a URL into the browser, the system first checks this file — if there's a mapping, it connects directly without going to DNS.
Details we'll cover in later chapters. Let's just look at the file contents.
Where's the file? Depends on OS:
Opening it, you'd see something like:
Many cracks include a step after installing where they ask you to modify this file, usually like:
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
in the browser, the system connects directly to
— your local machine. And remember, you installed a separate crack utility.

This crack utility runs on your local machine, so when you open programs like Office, the system connects to your local first. Result: it gets "tricked" into thinking validation and licensing succeeded, unlocking all features.
By now you should see: the real problem isn't modifying
. You have to run it to bypass validation, and viruses often hide in these crack programs. That's why people warn:
can sometimes break specific sites. So if you don't know what you're doing, don't touch it.
Many people are unclear on software licensing. The easiest confusion is between "shareware" and "freeware". They sound similar but mean different things. Quick walkthrough of common license types:
: completely free — free download, install, use. Usually closed-source, can't be modified or redistributed.
: free trial — usually time-limited or feature-limited; full features require payment. Long-term or full use requires buying a license.
: closed source. User only gets usage rights, can't modify or redistribute. Usually requires paid license.
: source open, free to use, modify, distribute — under specific licenses like GPL, AGPL, MPL, etc. "Free" here is about rights, not price.
: similar to Free Software, open source, modifiable, redistributable — emphasizes collaboration and development efficiency. Common licenses: MIT, Apache, BSD, etc.
A bit confusing? Table for clarity:
The "cracks" we mentioned mostly target "proprietary software". Cracking converts paid proprietary software into free versions for users.

Using pirated software really proves the saying: "The free thing is the most expensive". You feel great in the moment for saving money, but later — unstable computer, missing files, even legal risk — you end up paying more to clean it up.
